title: Corticosteroids — Equivalence & Weaning
oneLiner: Rapidly compare steroid potencies and safely taper therapy to avoid adrenal crisis; vital for any patient on systemic steroids >3 weeks.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Adrenal crisis (i.e., acute adrenal insufficiency):** Look for **hypotension + hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypoglycaemia** in any patient on or recently weaned from corticosteroids. This phenomenon kills by hypotension and electrolyte chaos. The single finding: **abrupt drop in BP after missing steroid dose / during illness**.
- **Steroid-induced severe infection (e.g., sepsis, disseminated varicella, aspergillosis):** Patients on high-dose steroids are immunocompromised. The single finding: **unexpectedly high fever (or absence of fever in sepsis) + raised CRP/procalcitonin** despite steroid use. Do not attribute “just” to steroid effect.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cushingoid features** (moon face, buffalo hump, central obesity, thin skin, striae, proximal myopathy, osteoporosis) — typical with prolonged high-dose use.
- **Adrenal insufficiency symptoms during weaning or after withdrawal:** fatigue, weakness, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, hypotension, abdominal pain, diarrhoea, confusion, hypoglycaemia.
- **Iatrogenic diabetes / glucose intolerance** — often first detected by hyperglycaemia on routine monitoring.
- **Steroid-induced psychosis or mood changes** — insomnia, agitation, hypomania (especially with prednisolone >40 mg/day).
- **Growth suppression in children** — careful height/weight monitoring.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison’s disease)** – usually pigmentation, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia; but in steroid-induced adrenal suppression you may have similar labs without pigmentation; short synacthen test differentiates.
- **Secondary adrenal insufficiency (hypopituitarism)** – also lacks pigmentation; may have other pituitary deficits (low LH/FSH, low TSH). **Clinical clue: if patient is on long-term high-dose steroids, assume it’s iatrogenic first.**
- **Sepsis without adrenal insufficiency** – may have hypotension but typically with fever, raised lactate, focal infection; consider synergistic immune suppression.
- **Hyperglycaemia (steroid-induced diabetes vs. pre-existing diabetes)** – check HbA1c, fasting glucose; steroids often cause post-prandial hyperglycaemia.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Check baseline **blood pressure** (standing/supine), **HR**, **temperature**, **capillary glucose** (all at risk).
  - **Blood culture** if febrile or suspected infection.
- **Bloods:**
  - **U&Es** (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia in adrenal insufficiency), **cortisol** (morning, 09:00 – but be aware that steroids interfere with assay).
  - **Synacthen (ACTH) stimulation test** (Short Synacthen Test – SST) when suspecting adrenal suppression:
    - Give 250 mcg IV/IM tetracosactide; measure cortisol at 0 and 30 minutes.
    - Normal: rise to >430–500 nmol/L (local lab reference); if blunted response, adrenal suppression.
  - **FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, glucose, lactate, blood cultures, varicella serology (if no history)**
- **Imaging:** Not first-line for steroid equivalence. Adrenal CT only if primary Addison’s suspected; chest XR/CT if suspected tuberculosis/fungal infection.
- **Special tests:** **Insulin tolerance test** (rarely needed) for HPA axis assessment.
- **Never-miss test:** **Short Synacthen Test (SST)** before labelling someone “steroid dependent” if you cannot stop steroids – but in acute illness, do not delay stress-dose steroids for the test.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Adrenal crisis** – give **hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat**, then **100 mg IV every 6 hours** (or continuous infusion) plus **0.9% saline 500 mL–1L bolus** (or dextrose if hypoglycaemia). Do NOT wait for cortisol results.
  - For non-crisis patients on high-dose steroids requiring coverage for surgery/sepsis: give **hydrocortisone 100 mg IV pre-procedure** then 50 mg IV 6 hourly for 24 h or until recovery.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Identify current steroid:** dose, duration, route. Convert to equivalent prednisolone daily dose (see table).
  - **Stress dosing** for febrile illness/injury: double oral dose (if mild illness) or switch to IV hydrocortisone (100 mg IV/6h) for major stress.
  - **Weaning plan** (see Drugs & Doses) – start only when acute illness resolved.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Gradual taper: reduce prednisolone by **2.5–5 mg/week** (or slower) until 5–7.5 mg, then slower (1 mg every 2–4 weeks) under endocrine supervision.
  - Always give “sick day rules” card (education: double dose during minor illness; if vomiting, use IM/SC hydrocortisone 100 mg and seek urgent medical help).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl 500 mL over 1–2 h for stress; maintenance IV fluids if NBM.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Yes (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless contraindicated.
  - **Diet:** normal (but avoid high salt if chronic steroid use? not standard). **NBM only if IV stress doses required?**
  - **Observations:** baseline NEWS2, BP lying/standing, glucose monitoring (pre-meal and bedtime if ongoing steroids; aim <10 mmol/L).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Equivalence table (as per BNF):**
  | Steroid                 | Equivalent glucocorticoid dose (relative to prednisolone) | Mineralocorticoid effect |
  |-------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------|
  | Prednisolone             | 5 mg oral = reference                                    | Minimal                  |
  | Hydrocortisone (oral)    | 20 mg oral = ~5 mg pred                                  | Significant              |
  | Hydrocortisone (IV)      | 100 mg IV/6h = ~20 mg pred/24h (cover for stress)        | Significant              |
  | Dexamethasone            | 0.75 mg oral = ~5 mg pred                                | None                     |
  | Betamethasone            | 0.75 mg oral = ~5 mg pred                                | None                     |
  | Methylprednisolone (IV)  | 4 mg IV = ~5 mg pred (approx conversion – check policy)  | Minimal                  |
- **First-line agents for weaning:**
  - For oral wean: **Prednisolone** (easiest to dose). For IV-to-oral switch: convert current IV HC to oral pred (e.g., HC 20 mg QDS = pred 15 mg BD?). Use local policy.
  - For adrenal suppression wean after long-term high-dose: **Hydrocortisone 15–20 mg in morning, 5–10 mg early evening** (more physiological).
- **Key contraindication:** **Systemic fungal infections** (except in organ transplant – then use with caution) – must treat infection first.
- **Interactions:**
  - NSAIDs (increased GI bleed risk) – avoid or PPI cover.
  - Warfarin/NOACs (increased anticoagulant effect).
  - Antiepileptics (e.g., carbamazepine, phenytoin) – increase steroid clearance; may need higher doses.
  - **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose change in renal failure. In severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), some steroids (e.g., prednisone conversion to prednisolone) may need decreased dose – check BNF.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Abrupt cessation of long-term steroids** (prednisolone >7.5 mg/day for >3 weeks) without wean **causes adrenal crisis with hypotension, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, and death.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Adrenal crisis (see Killers).
  - Hyperglycaemia (may require insulin).
  - Electrolyte disturbance (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia – more with hydrocortisone).
  - Psychosis / mania / depression.
  - Increased infection risk (especially VZV, TB, fungal).
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoporosis (BMD DXA scan indicated if ≥3 months of steroid use)
  - Diabetes mellitus.
  - Proximal myopathy.
  - Cushing’s syndrome features.
  - Cataracts / glaucoma.
  - Adrenal suppression (any dose >5 mg prednisolone/day for >3 weeks leads to risk). **Even after wean, HPA axis may take up to 12 months to recover.**
sections.Prognosis: - With appropriate weaning and stress-dose cover, most patients can discontinue steroids without permanent adrenal insufficiency. However, a significant minority (especially on >7.5 mg/day for >6 months) develop long-term dependence. Mortality from adrenal crisis is ~6% if untreated; almost zero if stress-dosing is done properly.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always give a “steroid card”** to any patient starting long-term treatment (>3 weeks) – document dose, duration, weaning plan, and sick day rules.
- **Prevent osteoporosis:** prescribe bisphosphonate (e.g., alendronic acid 70 mg once weekly) if planned corticosteroid course ≥3 months (NICE CG146). Give calcium/vitamin D (e.g., Adcal D3 1 tablet BD).
- **Monitor glucose:** check fasting glucose before starting high-dose steroids; then regular capillary glucose 2–4 hourly on ward; start insulin/ metformin if needed.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never stop steroids abruptly** without wean (unless <3 weeks’ duration on low dose).
- **Do not perform a short synacthen test** in a patient who is acutely unwell on steroids – you will get a false-normal result; just give stress doses.
- **Do not underestimate the effect of missing a single dose** – for patients on hydrocortisone 20 mg mane/10 mg nocte, a missed evening dose can lead to morning hyponatraemia.
- **Avoid using NSAIDs** in steroid-dependent patients without PPI cover – high risk of GI ulceration.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** fever, vomiting/diarrhoea (cannot take oral steroids), severe headache, confusion, abdominal pain, or if they miss a steroid dose.
- **Return if:** symptoms of hypoglycaemia (sweating, confusion, fast heart rate) – especially if on insulin for steroid-induced hyperglycaemia.
- **Follow-up:** with GP/endocrine clinic to continue wean and monitor BMD; if on >7.5 mg prednisolone for >3 months, refer to DXA service.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - Hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg) despite fluid bolus.
  - Hypoglycaemia (<3.0 mmol/L) not responding to oral/IV dextrose.
  - Electrolyte disturbance (Na <125, K >6.5) or increasing confusion.
  - Septic shock criteria (NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >2, organ failure).
- **To crash team:** cardiac arrest, impending respiratory failure, ongoing seizure.
- **Always think:** “Could this be missed adrenal insufficiency?” in any steroid-treated patient with hypotension, especially during sepsis or surgery.

rawMarkdown:
## Corticosteroids — Equivalence & Weaning
**One-liner:** Rapidly compare steroid potencies and safely taper therapy to avoid adrenal crisis; vital for any patient on systemic steroids >3 weeks.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Adrenal crisis (i.e., acute adrenal insufficiency):** Look for **hypotension + hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypoglycaemia** in any patient on or recently weaned from corticosteroids. This phenomenon kills by hypotension and electrolyte chaos. The single finding: **abrupt drop in BP after missing steroid dose / during illness**.
- **Steroid-induced severe infection (e.g., sepsis, disseminated varicella, aspergillosis):** Patients on high-dose steroids are immunocompromised. The single finding: **unexpectedly high fever (or absence of fever in sepsis) + raised CRP/procalcitonin** despite steroid use. Do not attribute “just” to steroid effect.

### Presentation
- **Cushingoid features** (moon face, buffalo hump, central obesity, thin skin, striae, proximal myopathy, osteoporosis) — typical with prolonged high-dose use.
- **Adrenal insufficiency symptoms during weaning or after withdrawal:** fatigue, weakness, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, hypotension, abdominal pain, diarrhoea, confusion, hypoglycaemia.
- **Iatrogenic diabetes / glucose intolerance** — often first detected by hyperglycaemia on routine monitoring.
- **Steroid-induced psychosis or mood changes** — insomnia, agitation, hypomania (especially with prednisolone >40 mg/day).
- **Growth suppression in children** — careful height/weight monitoring.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison’s disease)** – usually pigmentation, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia; but in steroid-induced adrenal suppression you may have similar labs without pigmentation; short synacthen test differentiates.
- **Secondary adrenal insufficiency (hypopituitarism)** – also lacks pigmentation; may have other pituitary deficits (low LH/FSH, low TSH). **Clinical clue: if patient is on long-term high-dose steroids, assume it’s iatrogenic first.**
- **Sepsis without adrenal insufficiency** – may have hypotension but typically with fever, raised lactate, focal infection; consider synergistic immune suppression.
- **Hyperglycaemia (steroid-induced diabetes vs. pre-existing diabetes)** – check HbA1c, fasting glucose; steroids often cause post-prandial hyperglycaemia.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Check baseline **blood pressure** (standing/supine), **HR**, **temperature**, **capillary glucose** (all at risk).
  - **Blood culture** if febrile or suspected infection.
- **Bloods:**
  - **U&Es** (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia in adrenal insufficiency), **cortisol** (morning, 09:00 – but be aware that steroids interfere with assay).
  - **Synacthen (ACTH) stimulation test** (Short Synacthen Test – SST) when suspecting adrenal suppression:
    - Give 250 mcg IV/IM tetracosactide; measure cortisol at 0 and 30 minutes.
    - Normal: rise to >430–500 nmol/L (local lab reference); if blunted response, adrenal suppression.
  - **FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, glucose, lactate, blood cultures, varicella serology (if no history)**
- **Imaging:** Not first-line for steroid equivalence. Adrenal CT only if primary Addison’s suspected; chest XR/CT if suspected tuberculosis/fungal infection.
- **Special tests:** **Insulin tolerance test** (rarely needed) for HPA axis assessment.
- **Never-miss test:** **Short Synacthen Test (SST)** before labelling someone “steroid dependent” if you cannot stop steroids – but in acute illness, do not delay stress-dose steroids for the test.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Adrenal crisis** – give **hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat**, then **100 mg IV every 6 hours** (or continuous infusion) plus **0.9% saline 500 mL–1L bolus** (or dextrose if hypoglycaemia). Do NOT wait for cortisol results.
  - For non-crisis patients on high-dose steroids requiring coverage for surgery/sepsis: give **hydrocortisone 100 mg IV pre-procedure** then 50 mg IV 6 hourly for 24 h or until recovery.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Identify current steroid:** dose, duration, route. Convert to equivalent prednisolone daily dose (see table).
  - **Stress dosing** for febrile illness/injury: double oral dose (if mild illness) or switch to IV hydrocortisone (100 mg IV/6h) for major stress.
  - **Weaning plan** (see Drugs & Doses) – start only when acute illness resolved.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Gradual taper: reduce prednisolone by **2.5–5 mg/week** (or slower) until 5–7.5 mg, then slower (1 mg every 2–4 weeks) under endocrine supervision.
  - Always give “sick day rules” card (education: double dose during minor illness; if vomiting, use IM/SC hydrocortisone 100 mg and seek urgent medical help).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl 500 mL over 1–2 h for stress; maintenance IV fluids if NBM.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Yes (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless contraindicated.
  - **Diet:** normal (but avoid high salt if chronic steroid use? not standard). **NBM only if IV stress doses required?**
  - **Observations:** baseline NEWS2, BP lying/standing, glucose monitoring (pre-meal and bedtime if ongoing steroids; aim <10 mmol/L).

### Drugs & Doses
- **Equivalence table (as per BNF):**
  | Steroid                 | Equivalent glucocorticoid dose (relative to prednisolone) | Mineralocorticoid effect |
  |-------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------|
  | Prednisolone             | 5 mg oral = reference                                    | Minimal                  |
  | Hydrocortisone (oral)    | 20 mg oral = ~5 mg pred                                  | Significant              |
  | Hydrocortisone (IV)      | 100 mg IV/6h = ~20 mg pred/24h (cover for stress)        | Significant              |
  | Dexamethasone            | 0.75 mg oral = ~5 mg pred                                | None                     |
  | Betamethasone            | 0.75 mg oral = ~5 mg pred                                | None                     |
  | Methylprednisolone (IV)  | 4 mg IV = ~5 mg pred (approx conversion – check policy)  | Minimal                  |
- **First-line agents for weaning:**
  - For oral wean: **Prednisolone** (easiest to dose). For IV-to-oral switch: convert current IV HC to oral pred (e.g., HC 20 mg QDS = pred 15 mg BD?). Use local policy.
  - For adrenal suppression wean after long-term high-dose: **Hydrocortisone 15–20 mg in morning, 5–10 mg early evening** (more physiological).
- **Key contraindication:** **Systemic fungal infections** (except in organ transplant – then use with caution) – must treat infection first.
- **Interactions:**
  - NSAIDs (increased GI bleed risk) – avoid or PPI cover.
  - Warfarin/NOACs (increased anticoagulant effect).
  - Antiepileptics (e.g., carbamazepine, phenytoin) – increase steroid clearance; may need higher doses.
  - **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose change in renal failure. In severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), some steroids (e.g., prednisone conversion to prednisolone) may need decreased dose – check BNF.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Abrupt cessation of long-term steroids** (prednisolone >7.5 mg/day for >3 weeks) without wean **causes adrenal crisis with hypotension, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, and death.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Adrenal crisis (see Killers).
  - Hyperglycaemia (may require insulin).
  - Electrolyte disturbance (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia – more with hydrocortisone).
  - Psychosis / mania / depression.
  - Increased infection risk (especially VZV, TB, fungal).
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoporosis (BMD DXA scan indicated if ≥3 months of steroid use)
  - Diabetes mellitus.
  - Proximal myopathy.
  - Cushing’s syndrome features.
  - Cataracts / glaucoma.
  - Adrenal suppression (any dose >5 mg prednisolone/day for >3 weeks leads to risk). **Even after wean, HPA axis may take up to 12 months to recover.**

### Prognosis
- With appropriate weaning and stress-dose cover, most patients can discontinue steroids without permanent adrenal insufficiency. However, a significant minority (especially on >7.5 mg/day for >6 months) develop long-term dependence. Mortality from adrenal crisis is ~6% if untreated; almost zero if stress-dosing is done properly.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always give a “steroid card”** to any patient starting long-term treatment (>3 weeks) – document dose, duration, weaning plan, and sick day rules.
- **Prevent osteoporosis:** prescribe bisphosphonate (e.g., alendronic acid 70 mg once weekly) if planned corticosteroid course ≥3 months (NICE CG146). Give calcium/vitamin D (e.g., Adcal D3 1 tablet BD).
- **Monitor glucose:** check fasting glucose before starting high-dose steroids; then regular capillary glucose 2–4 hourly on ward; start insulin/ metformin if needed.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never stop steroids abruptly** without wean (unless <3 weeks’ duration on low dose).
- **Do not perform a short synacthen test** in a patient who is acutely unwell on steroids – you will get a false-normal result; just give stress doses.
- **Do not underestimate the effect of missing a single dose** – for patients on hydrocortisone 20 mg mane/10 mg nocte, a missed evening dose can lead to morning hyponatraemia.
- **Avoid using NSAIDs** in steroid-dependent patients without PPI cover – high risk of GI ulceration.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** fever, vomiting/diarrhoea (cannot take oral steroids), severe headache, confusion, abdominal pain, or if they miss a steroid dose.
- **Return if:** symptoms of hypoglycaemia (sweating, confusion, fast heart rate) – especially if on insulin for steroid-induced hyperglycaemia.
- **Follow-up:** with GP/endocrine clinic to continue wean and monitor BMD; if on >7.5 mg prednisolone for >3 months, refer to DXA service.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - Hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg) despite fluid bolus.
  - Hypoglycaemia (<3.0 mmol/L) not responding to oral/IV dextrose.
  - Electrolyte disturbance (Na <125, K >6.5) or increasing confusion.
  - Septic shock criteria (NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >2, organ failure).
- **To crash team:** cardiac arrest, impending respiratory failure, ongoing seizure.
- **Always think:** “Could this be missed adrenal insufficiency?” in any steroid-treated patient with hypotension, especially during sepsis or surgery.
